text: China is one of the countries with the largest distribution of cold regions. The area of permanent permafrost and seasonal permafrost accounts for approximately 75% of the total land area of the country. With the increasing number of construction projects in cold regions in our country, a large number of freezing rock problems have emerged. However, up to now, people's research on freezing rock problems is very insufficient. In response to the increasing engineering demands in cold regions, this thesis separately presents the problem of frozen rock in cold regions as a new research direction. Centering on the issue of frozen rocks, this paper, based on experimental research, adopts a method combining theoretical analysis and numerical calculation to systematically study the mechanical and thermal properties of rocks under low-temperature and freeze-thaw cycle conditions. According to the experimental results, the macroscopic freeze-thaw damage constitutive relationship of rocks is established. Finally, taking the actual large-scale rock mass project in cold regions - the Kunlun Mountain Tunnel of the Qinghai-Xizang Railway as the background, the corresponding temperature is established The multi-field coupling mathematical models of degree-seepage and temperature-seepage - stress were adopted, and the finite element method was used to conduct practical calculation and analysis of frozen rock engineering in cold regions. In summary, the main research contents of this thesis are as follows: Two typical rocks (red sandstone and shale) are taken from the engineering site, processed into standard specimens (φ50mm×100mm), and uniaxial compression tests and triaxial compression tests are conducted at room temperature (20℃), different freezing temperatures, and different moisture states (fully saturated and dry). The freezing temperatures for the uniaxial compression test are 0℃,-5℃,-10℃, and -20℃ respectively, while those for the triaxial compression test are -5℃ and -10℃. The deformation and failure laws of specimens in uniaxial compression and triaxial compression tests, the stress-strain relationship, as well as the parameters c of uniaxial compressive strength, elastic modulus, triaxial compressive strength and triaxial shear strength under saturated and dry conditions were analyzed. The variation relationship of the value with temperature is presented, and the corresponding fitting relationship expression is given. Finally, ultrasonic wave velocity tests and thermal parameter tests were conducted on two types of rocks under different low temperatures and in both saturated and dry states, and the relationships between wave velocity, thermal conductivity and temperature were provided. Freeze-thaw cycle tests were conducted on red sandstone and shale under open saturated water conditions. One freeze-thaw cycle test included freezing in a low-temperature freezer at -20℃ for 12 hours and thawing in pure water at 20℃ at room temperature for 12 hours. The freeze-thaw damage and deterioration as well as the freeze-thaw failure behaviors of two types of rocks were analyzed. Two freeze-thaw damage and deterioration models represented by red sandstone and shale respectively were proposed: the spillage model and the crack model. Uniaxial compression tests were conducted on rock samples after different freeze-thaw cycles (0,5,10,20,30 times). The deformation and strength variation laws of the rocks after freeze-thaw cycles were recorded. The freeze-thaw durability of these two types of rocks was analyzed, and data fitting was carried out on the test results. The fitting relationship between the uniaxial compressive strength, elastic modulus and freeze-thaw cycles of the two types of rocks in the saturated state was obtained It is an expression. Based on this and in combination with the existing research data, an in-depth analysis was conducted on the mechanism of freeze-thaw damage and deterioration of rocks and its influencing factors, and the influence range of various factors was pointed out. Based on the uniaxial compression test results of rocks after freeze-thaw cycles and starting from the theory of macroscopic damage mechanics, the damage of rocks is divided into two stages. The first stage is the damage caused by freeze-thaw, and the second stage is the damage caused by freeze-thaw and uniaxial compression

This chapter systematically introduces the uniaxial compressive strength, triaxial compressive strength, deformation and failure laws of red sandstone and shale under different low-temperature environments and two extreme water-bearing states, as well as the variation laws of their acoustic wave velocity and thermal conductivity. The main conclusions are as follows: (1) Uniaxial compression tests and triaxial compression tests show that regardless of room temperature or low temperature, the stress-strain curves of red sandstone and shale all have four stages of deformation, and their failure is mainly shear failure. The shear failure surface is complete and forms a certain angle with the direction of the maximum principal stress. (2) The results of the uniaxial compression test show that the uniaxial compressive strength and elastic modulus of rocks in both saturated and dry states increase with the decrease of temperature, but the growth patterns are different. Overall, the influence of temperature variation on the uniaxial compressive strength and elastic modulus of red sandstone is greater than that of shale, and the influence of water-bearing state on the uniaxial strength of shale is significantly greater than that of red sandstone. The results of the triaxial compression test show that when the temperature is constant, the triaxial compressive strength of rock increases significantly with the increase of confining pressure, while when the confining pressure is constant, the compressive strength of rock increases with the decrease of temperature. The maximum principal strain of shale is significantly greater than that of red sandstone. The shear strength parameters of the two rocks, minimum values, both show an increasing trend with the decrease of temperature. However, due to the differences in lithology, porosity and water content, at different temperatures, the minimum values of red sandstone are more significantly affected by temperature than those of shale. (4) According to the ultrasonic test, the lower the temperature, the greater the longitudinal wave velocity of these two types of rocks, and the variation of the longitudinal wave velocity of saturated rocks is much greater than that of dry rocks. (5) According to the thermal conductivity determination test, the thermal conductivity of both types of rocks increases when the temperature drops from -20 °C to -10 °C. At the same temperature, the thermal conductivity of saturated rock is much greater than that of dry rock; under the same conditions, the thermal conductivity of red sandstone is greater than that of shale.

(1) Freeze-thaw cycle tests (up to 30 times) were conducted on red sandstone and shale under open saturation conditions. The freeze-thaw damage and deterioration processes of the two rocks were described in detail, and the mechanisms of freeze-thaw damage and deterioration of the two rocks were analyzed. Two freeze-thaw damage and deterioration models were proposed: the spillage model and the crack model. (2) Uniaxial compression tests were conducted on red sandstone and shale before and after different freeze-thaw cycles. Complete stress-strain curves of the rocks after different freeze-thaw cycles were obtained, and the strength and deformation laws of these two types of rocks were analyzed. It was found that the relationship between the elastic modulus and uniaxial compression strength of red sandstone and the number of freeze-thaw cycles can be fitted by quadratic polynomials, while the relationship between the elastic modulus and uniaxial compressive strength of shale and the number of freeze-thaw cycles can be fitted by an exponential decay function, with good fitting results. (3) By comparing the freeze-thaw durability of the two types of rocks, it was found that the freeze-thaw durability of red sandstone is very poor, while that of shale is average. In engineering, the influence of freeze-thaw needs to be considered for both types of rocks. (4) An in-depth analysis of the mechanism of rock freeze-thaw damage and deterioration was conducted. It was pointed out that the freeze-thaw damage of rocks is caused by the tensile strength of the rocks being insufficient to resist the freeze-thaw force. The fundamental mechanisms for the freeze-thaw damage and deterioration of rocks are the frost heaving of in-situ ice, water migration, the growth of separated ice, and the creep of ice. Finally, the influencing factors of freeze-thaw damage to rocks were discussed in detail from multiple aspects.

(1) Firstly, the damage of rocks in freeze-thaw environments is divided into two stages: the first stage is the damage caused by the number of freeze-thaw cycles, and the second stage is the total damage caused by the number of freeze-thaw cycles and the applied load. (2) Based on the test results of the previous chapter, different freeze-thaw damage evolution equations for the two types of rocks are established by analyzing the change in elastic modulus. Among them, the relationship between freeze-thaw damage of red sandstone and the number of freeze-thaw cycles can be expressed by a quadratic polynomial, while that of shale can be expressed by an exponential decay function. (3) As rock is a typical brittle material, according to the macroscopic phenomenological theory, the Lemaitre equivalent strain assumption and its corresponding damage model can be used to obtain the specific damage evolution equations and the corresponding damage stress-strain constitutive relationships of the two types of rocks under different freeze-thaw cycles. The stress-strain relationships calculated by the model are compared with the test curves, indicating that the established damage constitutive relationships are basically consistent with the test results. (4) The total damage of rocks is considered as the coupling of freeze-thaw damage and load damage. The total damage evolution equation and the total damage evolution rate equation of rocks in freeze-thaw environments are established, and the evolution curves of the total damage rate with strain of the two types of rocks under different freeze-thaw cycles are given. (5) Due to the complexity of the mechanisms and influencing factors causing freeze-thaw damage to rocks, the damage evolution of rocks in freeze-thaw environments is a complex mechanical problem. The total damage model established in this chapter only considers the influence of freeze-thaw cycles and strain, and does not take into account other influencing factors such as porosity, freeze-thaw temperature, or unsaturation. In addition, real rock masses are generally in a three-dimensional stress state. Therefore, how to extend the one-dimensional rock freeze-thaw damage model established in this paper to a three-dimensional situation still needs further research.

Based on the two-dimensional finite element calculation and analysis of the coupling of temperature and seepage under different conditions and only considering the temperature field at the shallow-buried section of the Kunlun Mountain Tunnel of the Qinghai-Xizang Railway, the following conclusions can be drawn: Under freeze-thaw conditions, seepage in tunnels has a significant impact on the temperature distribution of the surrounding rock. The greater the seepage rate, the greater the temperature change of the surrounding rock. Moreover, during the melting season, the corresponding melting depth of the tunnel surrounding rock is also greater. Therefore, the anti-seepage and water control work of tunnels is of great importance. (2) For the calculation model considered in this paper, due to the heat exchange between the tunnel top gullies and the tunnel walls and the environment, the annual temperature difference of the surrounding rock at the tunnel arch top is quite large, and there is always a melting phenomenon. Therefore, this area should be given priority treatment. (3) The function of the insulation layer is obvious. When there is an insulation layer, whether seepage is considered or not, no melting circle appears in the surrounding rock of the tunnel. (4) The tunnel construction environment causes significant temperature disturbances to the surrounding rock, but as time goes by, the temperature distribution of the surrounding rock gradually tends to a dynamically stable state. (5) When building tunnels in high-altitude areas, even if insulation layers are laid to prevent the freezing and thawing of the surrounding rock, the lining is inevitably in a freeze-thaw state. Therefore, the frost resistance and freeze-thaw durability of concrete are of vital importance.

This paper conducts statistics on the frost heaving rates of three types of subgrade soils with different plasticity indices after different freeze-thaw cycles, and analyzes and processes the test data. The rules of the frost heaving rates of different plastic subgrade soils after different freeze-thaw cycles are as follows: (1) Under the same freeze-thaw cycle number conditions, the frost heaving rate of subgrade soils increases with the increase of plasticity index; (2) For the same type of soil, the frost heaving rate of the subgrade soil increases with the increase in the number of freeze-thaw cycles. (3) The nonlinear fitting method was adopted to obtain the relationship between the frost heave rate of the subgrade soil and the plasticity index as well as the freeze-thaw cycle coefficient. It can be seen from the correlation coefficients that the fitting effect is ideal. This paper only conducted eight freeze-thaw cycles on three types of soils with different plasticity indices. The freeze-thaw rate patterns of other soils with different plasticity indices and those after more thaw cycles still need further research and verification.

The freezing and thawing process of soil is actually a phase change process of water within it: when the heat in the soil is released and its temperature drops to the initial freezing temperature of the soil, the water in the soil begins to freeze. Due to the different forms and types of soil moisture, its freezing process varies accordingly. The main types of moisture in the soil include: (1) Hygroscopic water. Hygroscopic water, also known as tightly bound water, is gaseous moisture adsorbed by dry soil particles from the soil air. Water molecules are highly attracted by the surface of soil particles and possess the properties of solid water. Their freezing point is approximately -°C, and they cannot move freely. (2) Film water. Film water, also known as loose bound water, is a layer of film-like liquid water formed outside the hygroscopic water layer when the hygroscopic water in the soil reaches its maximum amount. The attractive force it receives from soil particles is less than that of hygroscopic water, the freezing point is approximately -°C, and it has relatively high viscosity. (3) Capillary water. When the moisture content in the soil exceeds the maximum content of film water, free water forms that is not affected by the suction force of soil particles and has relatively large mobility. This water moves in the capillary pores due to the capillary force and is called capillary water. (4) Gravity water. When the soil moisture content reaches the field capacity, the excess water cannot be held by capillary forces and moves downward under the effect of gravity, thus forming gravity water.

When pure water freezes at 0°C under standard atmospheric pressure, its freezing point is called 0°C. The liquid water in the soil turns into solid ice first, ice crystals form around the small solid particles in the liquid, and then the water turns into ice. During this period, the temperature changes over time can roughly be divided into several stages: supercooling stage, temperature jump stage, constant stage, and descending stage. During the supercooling stage, the moisture in the soil is at a negative temperature but no ice crystals exist, and the temperature at which ice crystals form is lower than the freezing point. During the temperature jump stage, when the temperature drops to a certain extent, ice crystals start to form and release the latent heat of crystallization, causing the soil temperature to rise sharply. The constant stage is the process by which the soil moisture phase turns into ice. During the descending stage, as the water phase in the soil turns into ice, the thickness of the water film decreases, the binding energy of soil particles on water molecules increases, and the ion concentration in the aqueous solution rises, causing the soil temperature to continue dropping. Based on the characteristics of temperature jumps in the curve, the temperature at the highest and most stable point after the jump is obtained as the initial freezing temperature of water in the soil. The difference between this temperature and the freezing point of pure water is called the freezing point drop. The supercooling of water in the soil and its duration mainly depend on the soil moisture content and the cooling rate. When the soil temperature approaches 0°C, soil water can remain in a non-crystalline state for a long time. When the soil temperature is below 0°C and rapid cooling occurs, the undercooling temperature is high and the end time is early. When the soil moisture content is low and approaches the plastic moisture content, it is affected by the surface energy of soil particles, and the undercooling temperature will decrease. The spatial sequence of water freezing in soil is closely related to the binding force of soil particles on water molecules. According to the form of water existence and the attraction it is subjected to by soil particles, the order of soil water from near to far from soil particles can be classified as crystalline water (combined water), hygroscopic water, film water, capillary water, and gravitational water. Among them, crystalline water and partially hygroscopic water are also called strongly bound water, and their freezing temperature can be as low as -°C. The outer layer of water that is further away from the soil particles absorbs moisture, and all the water in the film is called weakly bound water. Its outer layer freezes at 0°C. The closer it is to the surface of the soil particles, the lower its freezing point, and it can only freeze completely below -°C. The freezing temperature of capillary water is relatively higher than that of the other two, but still slightly lower. The constant stage of soil freezing freezes into ice in the order of gravity water, capillary water, and film water (from the outer layer to the inner layer). The initial freezing temperature of soil is influenced by its own properties: the mineral chemical composition of soil particles, their dispersion, the composition and concentration of aqueous solutions, as well as external conditions such as pressure. For a given type of soil, its freezing temperature mainly depends on the moisture content and salt content in the soil. Indoor experiments show that the initial freezing temperature increases with the increase of moisture content, decreases with the increase of salt content, and the influence of soil salt content on the freezing temperature is greater than that of soil texture. When the water content is the same, the freezing temperature decreases as the particles become finer or the concentration of the pore solution increases. At the same time, when the water content is the same, the melting temperature of permafrost is always higher than the freezing temperature, and the soil undergoes several freeze-thaw cycles, with the melting temperature slightly increasing. The initial freezing or thawing temperature of soil is a fundamental indicator for determining the depth of soil freezing or thawing, and it correspondingly affects the temperature gradient in the soil. On the one hand, the moisture in the soil is affected by the surface energy of soil particles; on the other hand, water contains a small amount of solute. Therefore, the freezing temperature of soil is lower than that of pure water. The difference between them is called the drop in freezing point.

When soil freezes and thaws, the porosity, structure, and physicochemical properties of soil moisture all change and are strongly dependent on the temperature in the soil. The thermal conductivity of soil decreases linearly with the decrease of temperature, and the water conductivity of soil decreases with the decrease of temperature. This is because as the temperature drops, the water film around the soil particles in the permafrost freezes sequentially from the outside to the inside, the thickness continuously decreases, and the transport channel of unfrozen water is further reduced. The specific heat of the soil framework mainly depends on the mineral composition and organic matter content and is related to temperature. Soil moisture can flow from lower temperatures to higher temperatures under the influence of temperature, which has been verified by many experiments. The temperature condition also determines the phase change of water, which will significantly affect the infiltration rate of water and the release of latent heat. At this point, temperature becomes an important factor in heat balance and hydrothermal migration.

The soil moisture content under natural conditions during the freeze-thaw process varies at different points in space due to time and position, thus exhibiting different characteristics. According to the form of water existing in the soil and the magnitude of the attraction to soil particles, soil moisture is classified into four types: hygroscopic water, film-like water, capillary water, and gravity water. A notable feature of soil during the freezing and thawing process is the phase change between water and ice or between ice and water, which increases or decreases the potential energy between water molecules and causes an energy exchange process that releases or absorbs heat to the outside. In winter, as the temperature drops to the freezing point, the gravity water and capillary water (also known as free water) in the soil start to freeze first. Since free water accounts for a large proportion of the liquid water in the soil, the content of liquid water in the soil drops sharply at this time. After all the free water is frozen, part of the membranous water begins to freeze. The freezing of all the free water and part of the membranous water is an important part of the phase change of soil moisture, and this temperature range is referred to as the intense phase transition temperature zone. As the temperature continues to drop, the remaining membranous water gradually freezes, but the amount of phase change water in this part is extremely small. The temperature range of this phase change is called the freezing phase change temperature zone. The positive temperature zone where there is no phase transition is called the unphase transition temperature zone. The process of soil melting is the opposite of the freezing process. As the temperature rises, the first part of the phase change (ice turns into liquid water) is membranous water, followed by free water. Thus, the moisture content of liquid water in the soil keeps increasing with the rise in temperature until it melts completely. The seasonal freeze-thaw process of soil is divided into four stages: unstable slow freezing stage, rapid and stable freezing stage, unstable melting stage, and melting stage. The curve represents the development process of the freezing or thawing front of seasonal frozen soil during freeze-thaw, and also reflects the variation law of the phase change temperature field in time and space.

Due to the frequent changes in the external environment of the soil during winter and spring, freeze-thaw soil exhibits different characteristics in different periods. Under natural freeze-thaw conditions in the field, the position and thickness of the frozen soil layer relative to the surface are constantly developing and changing, and have the following characteristics: (1) Unstable freezing stage: In early winter, the outside temperature drops below 0°C at night and rises above 0°C during the day. The soil surface layer undergoes an intermittent freeze-thaw process of freezing at night and thawing during the day. At night, a thick frozen thin layer of the surface soil forms, which melts completely during the day. This stage continues until the heat absorbed by the soil during the day is insufficient to compensate for the heat dissipated at night; that is, the frozen thin layer of the surface soil does not melt during the day either. The characteristics of the frozen layer at this stage are: ① The frozen layer is mostly granular structure, ice crystals gather around the soil grains but are separated from each other; ② The freezing depth is thick, generally not exceeding [depth]; ③ The frozen layer forms at night and melts around noon the next day. The unstable freezing period in the experimental area is from [start date] to [end date]. (2) Stable freezing stage: At this time, the temperature stably passes through 0°C. As the temperature continues to drop and the surface negative accumulated temperature increases, the freezing depth rapidly and steadily increases. The total moisture content within the soil frozen layer significantly increases until the outside temperature reaches the later stage of continuous low temperature, but the daily maximum temperature is still negative. The characteristics of the soil frost layer during this period are as follows: ① The frost layer is dense; ② The liquid water content within the frozen layer continues to decrease; ③ The temperature has always been negative. The stable freezing period of the experimental area is from [start date] to [mid-year]. (3) Unstable melting stage: At this time, the outside temperature begins to rise. The duration of the temperature above 0°C during the day increases, but the temperature at night remains negative. The average daily temperature is above 0°C. The surface soil starts to undergo an intermittent freeze-thaw process of freezing at night and thawing during the day, and the freezing front continues to develop deeper until it reaches the maximum freezing depth. The characteristics of the soil frost layer during this period are as follows: ① The surface evaporation is enhanced, which reduces the moisture content of the surface soil. It is mostly granular frost layer or non-frozen dry soil layer; ② The thickness of the surface melting is approximately [depth]; ③ The maximum freezing depth occurs. The maximum freezing depth of the soil in the test area during the test year is [depth]. The occurrence time is from [date] to [date]. The unstable melting stage in the test area is from mid-year to late year. (4) During the melting stage, the outside temperature continues to rise. The depth of melting of the soil surface layer increases during the day, while the daily minimum temperature remains below 0°C. Therefore, a thick high-level frozen layer is formed on the surface at night. There is a certain thickness of melting layer between the high-level frozen layer and the original frozen layer (low-level frozen layer), forming a phenomenon of double frozen layer. At this time, the high-level freeze layer freezes at night and melts during the day. The upper layer of the low-level freeze layer is exposed to the effect of solar radiation, and the lower layer is exposed to the effect of geothermal energy. A bidirectional melting phenomenon occurs, and the high-level freeze layer does not completely melt until the lowest temperature stably exceeds 0°C. The melting of the low-level freeze layer continues until the entire frozen layer melts. The characteristics of this stage are: ① The thickness of the high-level frozen layer reaches approximately [depth], mostly granular structure; ② The thickness variation of the melting layer between the two frozen layers is within the range of [depth]; ③ The liquid water content within the frozen layer gradually increases; ④ Soil evaporation gradually increases. The stable melting stage of the experimental area is from the beginning to the end of the year.

(1) The difference in soil water potential between any two points in the soil is the driving force for water migration, and the soil water potential gradient is the driving force for water movement in frozen soil. The unfrozen water in frozen soil together with the pore water in the unfrozen soil segment constitutes the source for water migration. During the test period in Harbin, the maximum freezing depth and the groundwater depth indicate that the frozen soil layer is unsaturated. During freezing, the unfrozen water content at a certain depth decreases slowly over time, indicating that soil moisture at this depth is continuously migrating upward. At greater depths, the water content changes very little, indicating that the effects of phase change and water migration in the upper frozen layer have minimal influence on the lower soil layers. (2) During freeze-thaw, the unfrozen water content at different depths experiences a process of gradually decreasing content—reaching a minimum—followed by gradually increasing content. The unfrozen water content curve of soil presents a concave shape. In contrast, the total water content of soil profiles at different depths shows a sudden increase—remaining basically constant—followed by a sharp decrease. The total water content curve presents a convex shape. (3) In the freeze-thaw characteristic curve of soil moisture, the melting curve is basically consistent with or slightly lags behind the freezing curve. In the test area, the melting curves at certain depths are slightly higher than the freezing curves under the same ground temperature due to some rainfall during melting. The freeze-thaw curves at deeper depths show good consistency or slight lag, indicating that the frozen layer acts as a barrier, and external rainfall has minimal effect on deeper frozen soil. (4) Test data indicate that unfrozen water content maintains a dynamic equilibrium with negative temperature. When expressed in Kelvin, it can be described by a power function; when expressed in Celsius, it can be described by a quadratic polynomial. The quadratic polynomial shows a higher correlation. The soil in the test area is heterogeneous, and its freeze-thaw characteristic curves do not coincide. (5) Soil water potential at different depths exhibits a sudden large decrease at the freezing point—followed by relatively stable changes—and a sudden increase at the end of melting when reaching the freezing point. The curve is concave, which aligns well with changes in total soil water content. The freezing point is an important factor causing sudden changes in soil water potential, and the development of freeze depth at different times can be determined based on sudden changes in total soil water content or soil water potential. (6) During freeze-thaw, the total soil water content in the test area reaches a maximum near a certain depth. As soil depth increases, the total water content gradually decreases, then gradually increases near another depth. Soil water potential is lowest near a certain layer, and as depth increases, the water potential increases accordingly. This explains how the presence of a soil water potential gradient causes large upward water migration from lower layers, resulting in maximum total water content near that depth. (7) Soil water potential shows good consistency with unfrozen water content and soil temperature changes. Soil water potential exhibits a quadratic polynomial relationship with liquid water content.

(1) The vertical water content distribution of seasonal frozen soil during freeze-thaw changes with maximum values on both sides; The existence of frozen soil significantly increases the soil water storage capacity . The water storage capacity on the frozen layer during the thawing period provides a basis for spring agricultural production and water-saving irrigation. (2) The variation law of soil moisture content refers to the concept of characteristic moisture content during the soil freeze-thaw process: minimum moisture content before freezing, stable moisture content after freezing, peak moisture content during thawing, and stable moisture content after thawing. Combined with the qualitative analysis of the soil in the experimental area, the dynamic change law of soil moisture content within the depth during the freeze-thaw process was analyzed. Youdaoplaceholder0 As a field heterogeneous soil, the dry bulk density of the soil at different depths varies greatly  the spatial distribution law of different characteristic values of soil moisture content in the vertical direction does not increase with the increase of soil depth  moisture content , but shows that the characteristic value is the largest at the depth. Due to the migration of the lower liquid water to the upper frozen layer , the moisture content of the frozen soil increases , especially the moisture content of the soil from the surface layer to the depth range increases significantly. During the melting period, the presence of the permafrost layer  blocks the downward flow of moisture  moisture remains above the impermeable layer until it melts to that layer. The maximum freezing depth is . Water migration has little effect on the soil at the depth , and its moisture content changes very little. ⑶ During the freezing period, the soil evaporation decreases by . Especially during the stable freezing period, the daily evaporation in months is only about , while after the surface layer of the soil begins to melt, the evaporation increases by . Permafrost, as an impermeable layer,  has a very significant effect in inhibiting evaporation. ⑷ The infiltration process of seasonal frozen soil in the test area under freezing conditions is very similar to that of non-frozen soil , but the infiltration time reaches stability within about minutes. The soil infiltration rate significantly decreases during the stable freezing process  frozen soil has obvious seepage reduction characteristics  its infiltration capacity is far less than that of non-frozen soil. The stable infiltration rate of frozen soil is significantly lower than that of non-frozen soil  and decreases with the increase of frozen layer depth. The winter temperature in Harbin area is characterized by continuous ultra-low temperature , which causes soil to freeze and lead to soil frost heaving , resulting in cracks in frozen soil. ⑸ Seasonal frozen soil basically does not generate flow during the freezing period . During the thawing period, the flow generation methods include saturation flow generation, full storage flow generation, etc. During the stable freezing period, due to soil freezing and river drying up, the  runoff is relatively small. The  melting period often forms the first runoff peak of the year - the spring runoff peak. Permafrost, as an impermeable layer, can increase runoff  in late spring and early summer, retain precipitation in autumn and winter  in winter, and enhance the water storage capacity of the basin. The variation of runoff is basically inversely proportional to the thickness of permafrost.

The simulation of freeze-thaw depth, soil water storage capacity, and soil evaporation of seasonal frozen soil is a very important and long-standing difficult problem in the simulation of frozen soil hydrological characteristics. In this paper, empirical fitting combined with partial least squares regression, neural networks, and multiple linear regression data processing methods are adopted to simulate the variation process of freeze-thaw depth, soil water storage capacity, and soil evaporation of frozen soil. The discussion is as follows: (1) Establish the empirical relationship equation between freeze-thaw depth and surface negative accumulated temperature, as well as surface negative accumulated temperature, to simulate the freeze-thaw depth. Although the fitting result is very good, considering the single selection factor, it is impossible to comprehensively and accurately simulate the dynamic change process of freeze-thaw depth. Therefore, a freeze-thaw depth model based on partial least squares regression is established for each independent variable (surface negative accumulated temperature, surface negative product), and the correlation coefficients of temperature, soil accumulated temperature, etc., the extracted components, and the relationship between the independent variables and various meteorological factors have been well analyzed and explained. The accuracy of the model was further analyzed through auxiliary analysis. Studies proved that the comprehensive effect of the regression model is satisfactory and can be applied in the modeling of the freeze-thaw depth of seasonal frozen soil. (2) Establish an empirical relationship equation between the water storage capacity of freeze-thaw soil and its influencing factors (freeze-thaw depth, negative accumulated air temperature, and negative accumulated surface temperature). Although the linear correlation coefficient of the fitting results is very high, the selected factor is single. Considering the nonlinear characteristics of water storage capacity variation and adopting a neural network-based design, a neural network-based soil layer water storage capacity model, a frozen layer upper water storage capacity model, and a frozen layer water storage capacity model were established. Neural networks, through network training and learning, can effectively describe the dynamic randomness, trend, and other non-stationary characteristics of soil water storage capacity during freeze-thaw periods. This paper adopts the network learning rule with momentum term to improve training speed. The prediction results show that the established model of soil water storage capacity during freeze-thaw based on neural network has high reliability and prediction accuracy and can be used to predict the future soil water storage capacity in the Harbin area. (3) Meteorological factors (relative humidity, water pressure, air pressure, wind direction, wind speed, ground temperature, air temperature) were selected as independent variables. The least square method was used to fit the multiple regression model to simulate and predict soil evaporation. The results showed that the regression effect was significant and the model accuracy was good. It can be applied to the simulation and prediction of freeze-thaw soil evaporation in the seasonal permafrost area of Harbin.

This paper selects the high-altitude cold area of Harbin, where seasonal frozen soil phenomenon is widespread, as the experimental base. Based on the field experiments of winter freeze-thaw soil, the dynamic migration law of water, seasonal freeze-thaw law, and hydrological characteristics under frozen soil conditions in this area are studied. On this basis, a simulation model of frozen soil hydrological characteristics is established. The research results are as follows: The freezing and thawing processes of seasonal frozen soil in Harbin area strongly depend on soil temperature. By analyzing the daily and seasonal variations of surface and deep soil temperatures, the results show that soil temperature has the characteristics of daily and annual periodic fluctuations. Its variation decays exponentially with the increase of depth, that is, the farther the soil depth is from the surface, the smaller the temperature variation range. The closer to the soil surface, the more obvious the influence of external air temperature on soil temperature. The diurnal and seasonal variations of soil temperature lag behind the changes in air temperature. There is a significant correlation between the depth of soil freeze-thaw and the negative accumulated temperature of the surface and the negative accumulated temperature of the external air temperature. The seasonal freeze-thaw process can be divided into four stages: unstable freezing stage, stable freezing stage, unstable thawing stage, and thawing stage. The freezing period proceeds unidirectionally from top to bottom. During the melting period, it shows bidirectional melting, from the top to the bottom of the soil surface and from the bottom to the top of the maximum freezing depth. The freezing process is relatively slow and lasts for a longer time, while the melting process is relatively rapid and lasts for a shorter time. The soil in the test area is an unsaturated soil layer. Based on this, a freeze-thaw depth simulation model based on partial least squares regression was established. The negative accumulated temperature of air, the negative accumulated temperature of the surface, and the accumulated temperature of the soil were selected as independent variables. The prediction results of the model were good and it could be used for freeze-thaw depth modeling. During the freeze-thaw period, the moisture content of unfrozen water in the soil from the surface to the maximum freezing depth undergoes a process of gradually decreasing content, reaching the lowest point, and gradually increasing content, and the curve takes on a 'concave' shape. The total moisture content of soil profiles at different depths suddenly increases, the content remains basically unchanged, and then suddenly decreases. The curve takes on a 'convex' shape, and the difference between them represents the soil ice content. In the freeze-thaw characteristic curve of soil moisture, the melting curve is basically consistent with or slightly lags behind the freezing curve. The moisture content of the unfrozen water in the later stage of the surface water melting characteristic curve is higher than the corresponding temperature of the freezing curve, which is due to the influence of rainfall during the melting period. The deep freeze-thaw characteristic curves show good consistency or slight lag, indicating a significant impermeability effect of the frozen layer. The content of unfrozen water maintains a dynamic equilibrium with negative temperature. The correlation expressed by quadratic polynomials is superior to that expressed by power functions. The soil-water potential gradient is the driving force for water migration in permafrost. The unfrozen water in the frozen soil and the pore water jointly constitute the source of water migration. The soil water potential at different depths undergoes sudden changes at the freezing point. Freezing causes a decrease in the soil matrix potential and a sudden increase in the total water content. Therefore, the freezing point is an important cause of the sudden change in water potential. The presence of salt in agricultural soil causes the phase change temperature to be below 0℃. The soil water potential shows a good consistency with the content of unfrozen water and temperature changes and has a quadratic polynomial relationship with the liquid water content. The soil infiltration process under frozen conditions is similar to that of non-frozen soil, but the time required to achieve stable infiltration is significantly shortened, and it can stabilize in about a few minutes. The infiltration rate decreases during the stable freezing process. The seepage reduction of frozen soil is significant, and the infiltration capacity is much lower than that of non-frozen soil, and it decreases with the increase of the depth of the frozen layer. Continuous low temperatures cause the soil to swell and develop cracks. Seasonal frozen soil basically does not generate runoff during the freezing period, and during the thawing period, the flow generation methods include both saturated flow generation and full storage flow generation. During the period of stable freezing, rivers dry up and have a small runoff. The melting period forms the peak of spring runoff. Permafrost, as an impermeable layer, can increase runoff in late spring and early summer and retain precipitation in winter, thereby enhancing the water storage capacity of the basin. The variation of runoff is basically inversely proportional to the thickness of permafrost. During the freeze-thaw period, the vertical moisture content of the soil reaches its maximum at a certain depth. Introduce the concept of characteristic moisture content: minimum moisture content before freezing, stable moisture content after freezing, peak moisture content during thawing, and stable moisture content after thawing. The vertical spatial distribution of the characteristic value moisture content does not increase with the increase of depth but shows the maximum at a certain depth. Permafrost significantly increases soil water storage capacity. The water storage capacity of the permafrost layer during the thawing period can provide a basis for spring agricultural production and water-saving irrigation. A neural network-based soil layer water storage capacity model, a frozen layer water storage capacity model, and a frozen layer water storage capacity model were established. The prediction results show that the models have high reliability and accuracy and can be used to predict future soil water storage capacity. During the freezing period, the evaporation of soil decreases, especially during the stable freezing period when the daily evaporation is only at a relatively low level, while the evaporation increases at the initial stage of surface melting. Permafrost, as an impermeable layer, has a significant inhibitory effect on evaporation. By selecting meteorological factors (relative humidity, water pressure, air pressure, wind direction, wind speed, ground temperature, and air temperature) as independent variables and using a multiple regression model to simulate soil evaporation, the regression effect is significant and the model accuracy is good. It can be used for the simulation and forecast of freeze-thaw soil evaporation in the seasonal permafrost area of Harbin.

Soil thermal state is a comprehensive product of energy and material exchange between the atmosphere and the land surface. Soil temperature is not only a sensitive climate indicator but also plays a significant role in geophysical and microbial processes. Understanding the thermal state of soil and its interrelationship with other environmental factors has received increasing attention. Research shows that there are regional and seasonal differences in global temperature changes: some believe that the polar regions warm up the fastest, some think that high-altitude areas warm up even faster, and some studies point out that arid and semi-arid regions warm up at a higher rate. The key issue lies in how to describe the global differences in land surface temperatures, the impact of these differences on soil thermal states, and which climatic factors dominate the changes in soil thermal states. To this end, this study combines grid-based climate data, meteorological station data and numerical models to analyze the regional differences of climate change from a new perspective and explore the responses of near-surface soil freeze-thaw and permafrost to climate change.

Global-scale analysis shows that when the average state of temperature and climate from 1961 to 1990 is used to describe the changes in global land surface temperature from 1951 to 2013, 81% of the world's land surface area has a multi-year average temperature ranging from -15℃ to 26℃. The annual average temperature increase in these regions is linearly related to the multi-year average temperature: for every 1℃ increase in the multi-year average temperature, the annual average temperature increase decreases by 0.028℃. On a monthly scale, in colder regions (with an annual average temperature ranging from -20℃ to 10℃), the temperature increase can reach up to 2.8℃ during the cold season (December to February of the following year), and this trend can last for up to 8 months (October to May of the following year), showing a significant warming trend. The area with an annual average temperature lower than 0℃ is defined as the cold zone. It is found that the temperature increase in the cold zone is approximately 1.68±0.19℃, while the temperature increase in the areas outside the cold zone during the same period is only 1.04±0.09℃.

At the regional scale, the freeze-thaw time of near-surface soil and its climate response were analyzed using the 0-centimeter soil temperature observation data from 636 ground stations in China from 1956 to 2006. The average state of the climate shows that the Qinghai-Xizang Plateau has a high altitude and the longest freezing period, and freezing may occur almost every day of the year. The freezing period in the high-latitude regions of Northeast China lasts approximately from September to June of the following year. The freezing time in eastern China is latitudinal zonal, while in the west it is mainly affected by altitude and topography. There are differences in the relationship between the number of freezing days and the distribution of permafrost: the boundary of the high-altitude permafrost area coincides with the 260-day freezing contour, while the boundary of the high-latitude permafrost area in Northeast China coincides better with the 220-day contour. There are differences between the near-surface soil freezing days retrieved by microwave remote sensing and the data from ground stations: regions with a multi-year average freezing day of less than 66 days are overestimated, while the rest of the regions are underestimated. The greatest differences are concentrated around the Qinghai-Xizang Plateau.

From 1956 to 2006, the start time of near-surface soil freezing in China was delayed by about 5 days, the end time was advanced by about 7 days, the freezing period was shortened by about 12 days, and the number of freezing days was reduced by about 10 days. The temperature rise in spring was more obvious than that in autumn. In the high-altitude areas of the west, the changes are faster. For every 1,000 meters of uplift, the start of freezing is delayed by 0.095 days per year, and the number of freezing days is reduced by 0.035 days per year. The variation range of freezing time in the east is negatively correlated with latitude: for every 1° increase in latitude, the variation ranges of freezing end time, freezing period length and freezing days decrease by 0.005, 0.006 and 0.007 days per year respectively. Urban expansion also has an impact on the number of freezing days. In areas with rapid expansion, the reduction rate is approximately -0.27 days per year, while in areas with slow expansion, it has changed significantly since the 1990s, with a slope of approximately -0.85 days per year.

The temperature variation of permafrost plays an important indicative role in climate change. The soil temperature at five standard depths (0.20, 0.40, 0.80, 1.60, and 3.20 meters) has a good relationship with altitude and latitude, with latitude being dominant, and can explain 50% to 70% of the spatial distribution characteristics. From 1961 to 2014, the soil temperature at all depths increased, and the increase in temperature decreased with the depth (0.38-0.24℃/10 years). The simulation results show that the temperature change in the cold season has the greatest impact on soil temperature, followed by snow cover depth, while the temperature in the warm season has the least impact. The thickness of the active layer is most sensitive to temperature changes in the warm season, while the influence of temperature and snow cover changes in the cold season is asymmetric. Overall, the colder the region, the faster the temperature rises. The thermal state of the soil near the surface shows obvious seasonal differences, with a significant increase in temperature in winter. This may prolong the growth period of vegetation in cold regions, change the structure of the ecosystem, and thereby affect the energy and water exchange between the surface and the atmosphere. Snow cover changes play a key role in the prediction of soil thermal state and climate feedback, and the interaction among temperature, snow cover and soil thermal state needs to be comprehensively considered.
